summary: Raw garage‑blues set with standout Viola Lee Blues and Dancin’ In The Street
blurb: The show delivers raw early‑summer blues energy, highlighted by Jerry’s spoken intro on Viola Lee Blues and Pigpen’s organ texture. Standout numbers include Dancin’ In The Street, He Was A Friend Of Mine, and Big Boss Man. Recording quality is good for a 1966 soundboard, making it an essential listen.
review: On July 3, 1966 at the Fillmore Auditorium the Dead were in full garage‑blues mode, ripping through a set that feels both youthful and surprisingly tight. Jerry’s early vocals are gritty yet melodic, and his trippy spoken intro to “Viola Lee Blues” sets a psychedelic tone that Pigpen amplifies with his raw organ growl. The rhythm section—Phil on bass and Bill Kreutzmann on drums—locks into a solid groove, especially on the driving “Big Boss Man” and “Viola Lee Blues,” while Bob Weir adds steady lead lines throughout. Highlights such as “Dancin’ In The Street,” “He Was A Friend Of Mine,” “Next Time You See Me,” and “I Know You Rider” showcase a band that’s still finding its voice but already capable of electrifying the room. Both available soundboard tapes capture this energy with crisp vocals and respectable clarity despite the inevitable tape‑age artifacts, making the recording an excellent snapshot of the Dead’s early summer ’66 sound.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Both recordings consistently praise the performance, calling it excellent, raw, and energetic with multiple standout songs mentioned (Viola Lee Blues, Dancin' In The Street, He Was A Friend Of Mine, etc.). No negative comments appear across analyses, indicating strong positive sentiment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent early‑1966 soundboard capture with crisp vocals, raw energy and the most comprehensive list of standout tracks.

key_highlights: Raw garage‑blues intensity that captures the Dead’s early summer vibe
Jerry’s spoken intro on “Viola Lee Blues” and youthful guitar work
Pigpen’s psychedelic organ texture adding depth to the blues numbers
Solid rhythm foundation from Phil and Bill, especially on “Big Boss Man”
song_highlights: He Was A Friend Of Mine
Sitting On Top Of The World

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers trippy spoken intro on “Viola Lee Blues,” early vocal grit and youthful guitar energy throughout the set.
band_performance.Keys: Pigpen’s organ adds a psychedelic texture that lifts the blues numbers, especially evident on “Viola Lee Blues.”
band_performance.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann stands out with a driving beat on “Viola Lee Blues” and keeps a tight groove across the entire set.

must_listen_sequences: He Was A Friend Of Mine
Sitting On Top Of The World
